contentBlocks.title: The ADME Detective: A 60-Second Bedside Routine
contentBlocks.htmlReady:
Use this any time a patient response feels ‘off’—too much effect, too little effect, or delayed toxicity.

  1. Confirm the basics: right patient, right drug, right dose, right route, right time, right formulation (IR vs ER/DR), last dose time.
  2. Assess the response: what is the expected effect (pain score change, HR/BP change, glucose change, symptom relief), and what are the adverse-effect signals (sedation, bleeding, hypotension, arrhythmia, GI symptoms)?
  3. Run ADME:
    • A (Absorption): vomiting/diarrhea? NPO? tube feeds? crushed ER tablet? food timing?
    • D (Distribution): edema/ascites? dehydration? low albumin? obesity/cachexia? pregnancy?
    • M (Metabolism): new inhibitor/inducer? new supplement? liver disease?
    • E (Excretion): declining urine output? rising creatinine? dialysis? dehydration?
  4. Escalate early with a hypothesis: “This looks like accumulation,” “This looks like malabsorption,” or “This looks like an interaction.”

sections.content:
2.0 Overview & How to Use This Chapter

Chapter 2 introduces pharmacokinetics: what the body does to a drug after the medication is administered. In bedside language, this chapter explains why the same medication order can produce good symptom control in one patient, no effect in another, and toxicity in a third. The difference is often not the drug itself. The difference is how that patient absorbs, distributes, metabolizes, and excretes the medication.

The chapter is organized around the ADME framework:

  1. Absorption: how the drug gets into the bloodstream
  2. Distribution: where the drug travels after it is absorbed
  3. Metabolism: how the body chemically changes the drug
  4. Excretion: how the drug and its metabolites leave the body

That sequence may sound theoretical at first, but it is one of the most practical frameworks in nursing pharmacology. When a patient response does not match expectations—too little effect, too much effect, delayed toxicity, or a sudden interaction—ADME gives you a disciplined way to think through the problem instead of guessing.

For nursing students, the goal is not to memorize isolated facts. The goal is to build a usable mental model. Safe medication practice depends on more than the “rights” of administration. It also depends on recognizing whether the patient’s current condition makes the ordered dose, route, or interval less safe than it looked on paper.

2.0.1 How to read this chapter like a clinician

A helpful way to study pharmacokinetics is to keep returning to one clinical question: What would I assess, monitor, teach, document, or report differently because of this concept? If a patient has vomiting, tube feeds, low albumin, cirrhosis, acute kidney injury, or a new interacting medication, pharmacokinetics changes. Nursing care should change too.

Use a three-pass approach as you move through the chapter:

  • Pass 1: Build the map. Make sure you can explain ADME in plain language.
  • Pass 2: Link each PK concept to bedside action—what you would check before the dose, what you would watch after the dose, and what would make you escalate.
  • Pass 3: Focus on the high-yield patterns that commonly cause harm: route/formulation errors, low albumin, renal decline, CYP interactions, and narrow therapeutic index drugs.
2.0.2 Why this chapter matters in real nursing work

Nurses often recognize pharmacokinetic problems first because nurses see the patient continuously and see the timeline. You are the person most likely to notice that a patient who tolerated dose one becomes progressively sedated after dose three, that the oral medication is not working because the patient is vomiting, or that a new antibiotic coincided with a rising INR.

Those observations matter because many medication problems are not random adverse reactions. They are explainable changes in drug exposure. Once you frame the problem correctly, the next step becomes clearer: hold and clarify, adjust timing, verify formulation, review recent medication changes, check organ function, or escalate concern about accumulation or therapeutic failure.

2.0.3 A practical chapter mindset: think therapeutic window

Most medications have a range where they are likely to help without causing unacceptable harm. That range is the therapeutic window. Pharmacokinetics helps explain why patients move into or out of that window. A dose can become too weak if absorption is poor or clearance is unusually fast. A dose can become too strong if protein binding falls, metabolism slows, or renal excretion declines.

Clinically, that means medication safety is dynamic. It changes when the patient changes. Sepsis, dehydration, heart failure, liver dysfunction, dialysis, pregnancy, frailty, malnutrition, and polypharmacy can all alter the way a drug behaves from one day to the next.

2.0.4 What you should be able to do after this chapter

By the end of Chapter 2, you should be able to look at an unexpected medication response and ask a sharper question than “Why is this drug acting weird?” You should be able to ask:

  • Could this be an absorption problem?
  • Could this be a distribution problem related to albumin, body composition, edema, or barriers?
  • Could this be a metabolism problem caused by liver disease, genetics, or an inhibitor/inducer?
  • Could this be an excretion problem caused by acute or chronic renal impairment?

That habit is the real purpose of the chapter. Once you can use ADME to explain what you are seeing, your assessments become more focused, your patient teaching becomes more useful, and your communication with the provider and pharmacy team becomes much stronger.

contentBlocks.title: Monitoring Intensity: Match the Risk to the Drug
contentBlocks.htmlReady:
A practical nursing rule: the narrower the therapeutic window and the slower the clearance, the more deliberate you must be about monitoring.

  • Wide window + fast clearance: monitor symptoms and basic vitals; toxicity is less likely to be delayed.
  • Narrow window: monitor symptoms and objective signals (labs, ECG changes, drug levels when ordered).
  • Long half-life / slow clearance: expect delayed changes after dose adjustments; watch for accumulation over days.

Documentation tip: when escalating a concern, include the timeline (last dose time, symptom onset time) because PK is time-dependent.

sections.content:
2.1 Pharmacokinetics Overview (ADME)

2.1.1 What pharmacokinetics means in nursing practice

Pharmacokinetics (PK) describes what the body does to a drug over time. The central nursing question is straightforward: How much drug is reaching the bloodstream and tissues, how fast is it getting there, and how long is it staying there? The answer helps predict whether the patient will experience therapeutic benefit, no effect, or toxicity.

PK is closely paired with pharmacodynamics (PD), which describes what the drug does once it reaches its target. In practice, nurses use both at the same time. PK helps explain drug exposure; PD helps explain drug response. Many bedside medication problems that appear to be “drug effect” problems are actually PK problems underneath. A pain medication may seem ineffective because it was not absorbed. A sedative may seem “too strong” because it accumulated after clearance declined.

2.1.2 The ADME framework

Pharmacokinetic processes are usually organized into four linked steps:

  1. Absorption — movement of the drug from the site of administration into the systemic circulation
  2. Distribution — movement of the drug from the bloodstream into tissues and body compartments
  3. Metabolism — chemical transformation of the drug, usually to facilitate elimination or alter activity
  4. Excretion — removal of the drug and its metabolites from the body

Although ADME is taught as a sequence, it functions more like a connected system. A change at one step often affects the others. A patient with shock may have reduced gut perfusion, altered tissue distribution, impaired hepatic metabolism, and evolving renal dysfunction all at once. This is why acute illness makes medication behavior less predictable.

2.1.3 The concentration-time curve: why timing matters

PK is often visualized as a concentration-time curve. After a dose is given, drug concentration rises, reaches a peak, and then declines as distribution and elimination occur. Nurses do not need to memorize the shape of every curve, but they do need to understand the decisions that depend on it:

  • Onset: when should the medication begin working?
  • Peak: when is the effect or toxicity risk likely to be strongest?
  • Duration: how long should the effect last?
  • Accumulation: if repeated doses are given, is the concentration gradually rising?

This is one reason medication timing is a safety skill rather than a clerical task. A blood pressure medication, insulin dose, pain medication, anticoagulant, or anticonvulsant can all behave very differently if timing, route, food intake, or organ function changes.

2.1.4 Core PK terms that shape bedside reasoning

Bioavailability (F) refers to the fraction of an administered dose that reaches the systemic circulation in active form. IV medications have 100% bioavailability because they bypass absorption barriers. Oral medications usually have lower and more variable bioavailability.

Clearance (CL) describes the body’s ability to remove drug from the plasma. Clearance is often influenced most by liver metabolism and kidney excretion. Reduced clearance means the same maintenance dose can produce higher exposure over time.

Half-life (t½) is the time required for plasma concentration to fall by about half. Half-life helps predict how often a medication should be dosed, how long it will take to wear off, and how long it may take to accumulate or reach steady state.

Volume of distribution (Vd) is a conceptual measure of how extensively a drug leaves the bloodstream and distributes into tissues. Drugs with a large Vd are more widely distributed; drugs with a small Vd remain more concentrated in the vascular space.

2.1.5 Why PK changes quickly in unstable patients

Pharmacokinetic predictions are easiest when the patient is stable. They become less reliable during rapid physiologic change. Sepsis, dehydration, hemorrhage, major fluid shifts, liver failure, heart failure, or acute kidney injury can all move the patient out of the expected pattern. In those settings, the nurse should think less in terms of “standard dose” and more in terms of current drug handling capacity.

  • Poor gut perfusion or active GI loss can reduce oral absorption.
  • Low albumin, edema, or obesity can alter distribution.
  • New inhibitors or inducers can change metabolism.
  • Oliguria, rising creatinine, or dialysis can change excretion.

That is why ADME is useful at the bedside: it converts a vague concern into a focused hypothesis.

2.1.6 Nursing implications of exposure, timing, and accumulation

The key PK concept linking all sections of this chapter is exposure. Effectiveness and toxicity are both driven by how much active drug the patient is exposed to over time. Exposure rises when absorption increases, protein binding changes, metabolism slows, or excretion falls. Exposure falls when the opposite occurs.

For nurses, that means three habits matter:

  • Verify the mechanics: right route, right formulation, right timing, and right administration technique
  • Assess the context: kidney function, liver disease, fluid status, nutrition, pregnancy, age, polypharmacy, and recent clinical change
  • Watch the pattern: no effect, delayed effect, progressive sedation, unexpected bleeding, recurrent pain, rising drug levels, or other clues that exposure shifted

When you understand PK this way, it stops being abstract. It becomes a practical framework for preventing harm, recognizing therapeutic failure early, and explaining medication concerns in clinically meaningful language.

contentBlocks.title: Enteral/Oral Absorption Checklist (Use Before You Give the Next Dose)
contentBlocks.htmlReady:
• GI status: vomiting, diarrhea, ileus, severe constipation, GI bleed, malabsorption history?
  • NPO status: is the patient actually allowed PO? If NPO, is the medication essential and is an alternate route ordered?
  • Food/tube feeds: should this be given with food, without food, or separated from feeds?
  • Formulation: is it IR vs ER/DR/EC? If crushed for a tube, is crushing permitted?
  • Perfusion: is the patient in shock, on high-dose vasopressors, or poorly perfused (which can reduce gut absorption)?
  • Interactions: antacids, supplements, calcium/iron, or other meds that should be spaced?

If any answer raises concern: pause and clarify the route, timing, or formulation rather than assuming the dose will behave normally.

sections.content:
2.2 Absorption & Bioavailability

Absorption is the process by which a drug moves from its site of administration into the bloodstream. In plain clinical language, absorption helps answer two practical questions: How much of this dose actually gets into the body? and How quickly will it get there? Those questions matter because a medication can be ordered correctly and still produce too little effect, delayed effect, or an unexpectedly strong effect if absorption changes.

For nurses, absorption is not just a pharmacology definition. It is a safety concept. When a patient is vomiting, receiving continuous tube feeds, lying on a heating pad over a transdermal patch, or swallowing a medication incorrectly, the issue is often not the prescribed drug itself. The issue is that the dose did not enter the body in the expected way.

2.2.1 Route determines how predictable absorption will be

The route of administration is the first major determinant of absorption. Intravenous (IV) medications bypass the absorption step entirely and enter the bloodstream directly, which is why IV bioavailability is considered 100%. By contrast, oral, sublingual, buccal, intramuscular, subcutaneous, inhaled, rectal, topical, and transdermal medications all depend on additional physiologic steps before full drug effect can occur.

From a nursing perspective, each route comes with its own risk pattern. Oral medications are vulnerable to food effects, vomiting, tube-feed interactions, altered GI motility, and first-pass loss. IM and SQ medications depend on local blood flow, so shock, hypothermia, edema, and vasoconstriction can make them less reliable. Transdermal medications are designed for slow, steady uptake, but heat and skin integrity can increase delivery unexpectedly. Inhaled medications may work beautifully or poorly depending almost entirely on patient technique.

2.2.2 Bioavailability: how much of the dose reaches systemic circulation

Bioavailability refers to the fraction of an administered dose that reaches the systemic circulation unchanged. It is easiest to understand by comparing routes. If 10 mg IV produces full systemic exposure, an oral dose of the same medication may need to be much higher because some of the swallowed drug is not absorbed or is metabolized before reaching the rest of the body.

Bioavailability is reduced by several mechanisms: incomplete absorption across the GI tract, drug degradation before absorption, metabolism in the intestinal wall, and first-pass metabolism in the liver. This is why an IV-to-PO conversion is not simply a matter of keeping the same number of milligrams. The route changes the exposure.

2.2.3 First-pass metabolism and why route matters

When a medication is swallowed, absorbed drug enters the portal circulation and travels through the liver before reaching the systemic circulation. If the liver metabolizes a substantial amount during that first pass, the circulating active dose becomes much smaller. This is called first-pass metabolism.

Some medications are intentionally given by routes that reduce or bypass first-pass loss, such as sublingual, buccal, transdermal, or IV administration. A classic bedside teaching point is that a sublingual medication should not be swallowed unless the product instructions specifically allow it. If the patient swallows it, onset may be delayed and the effect may be weaker because the drug now behaves like a swallowed oral dose.

2.2.4 Oral absorption depends on several steps, not just swallowing

For a swallowed medication to work as expected, several things must happen in sequence. The dosage form must disintegrate if appropriate, the drug must dissolve in GI fluid, it must reach an absorptive surface—usually the small intestine—and it must cross into the bloodstream. After that, gut-wall and hepatic metabolism may still reduce the amount that reaches systemic circulation.

This is one reason nurses should avoid thinking of oral administration as automatic. A dose may be documented as given, yet actual drug exposure may still be poor. If the patient pockets the tablet, vomits soon after swallowing, has severe diarrhea, has ileus, or receives the drug in a formulation that should not be crushed, the medication may not behave the way the MAR suggests.

2.2.5 Food, gastric pH, and GI motility

Food can slow gastric emptying, alter stomach pH, reduce absorption by binding with some medications, or improve absorption for certain lipophilic drugs. That is why some medications are labeled “take with food,” while others are labeled “take on an empty stomach.” These instructions are usually about predictable pharmacokinetics, not about being overly fussy.

GI motility matters too. Very rapid transit, such as significant diarrhea, may shorten contact time and reduce absorption. Very slow transit, such as ileus, severe constipation, opioid-related gut slowing, or anticholinergic effects, may delay onset and make response less predictable. If a patient insists that an oral medication “never kicks in until much later,” altered motility may be part of the explanation.

2.2.6 Vomiting, malabsorption, and critical illness

Vomiting can prevent a medication from remaining in the GI tract long enough to be absorbed. Chronic malabsorption syndromes and altered GI anatomy can do the same more gradually. Bariatric surgery may reduce stomach size, change gastric emptying, and alter where medications dissolve and absorb. Older adults may also have less reliable oral absorption because of dysphagia, reduced saliva, constipation, polypharmacy, or frequent use of acid-suppressing therapy.

In critical illness, the gut can become an especially unreliable route. Poor splanchnic perfusion in shock or severe heart failure may reduce oral or enteral absorption enough that a dose is technically given but not dependably delivered. In unstable patients who need rapid or predictable effect, that is a strong reason to escalate early and consider whether IV therapy is more appropriate.

2.2.7 Formulation matters: IR, ER, DR, and EC

The dosage form is part of the medication order, not an optional packaging detail. Immediate-release (IR) products are designed to dissolve and absorb relatively quickly. Extended-release (ER, XR, SR, CR) products are designed to release drug gradually over time. Delayed-release (DR) and enteric-coated (EC) products are designed to resist dissolution in the stomach and release later, often in the intestine.

Crushing, splitting, or opening a product that was designed for modified release can change absorption dramatically. That may lead to dose dumping, where the patient receives a large amount too quickly, increasing toxicity risk. It may also destroy the intended protection against stomach acid or gastric irritation. Nursing practice should therefore treat formulation verification as a safety step, especially when a patient has dysphagia or an enteral tube.

2.2.8 Drug-food and drug-drug interactions in the gut

Some interactions happen before the drug ever reaches the bloodstream. Medications may bind with calcium, iron, magnesium, or tube feeds and form complexes that reduce absorption. Acid suppression therapy can change absorption of medications that depend on a certain pH. Other medications can speed or slow motility enough to alter onset or total exposure.

This is why administration timing matters. If a medication guide instructs nurses to separate a dose from antacids, supplements, or feeds, that instruction usually exists to protect absorption. For nursing students, the practical lesson is simple: spacing is not a suggestion if the goal is reliable exposure.

2.2.9 Enteral tubes: a common high-risk absorption problem

Enteral administration introduces its own set of absorption and formulation challenges. Some drugs adhere to tubing, some interact with continuous feeds, some require an empty-stomach state that tube feeding prevents, and some should never be crushed at all. The safest habit is to verify whether the formulation is appropriate for the tube, follow flushing policy carefully, and coordinate feed interruptions only when clinically necessary and specifically recommended.

At the bedside, nurses often detect tube-related absorption problems before anyone else. Clues include a medication that suddenly becomes ineffective after a feeding schedule changes, recurrent tube occlusion, or a patient who appears to receive the dose on time but never shows the expected response.

2.2.10 Other route-specific bedside examples

IM and SQ medications depend on tissue perfusion. In a stable patient, absorption may be reasonably predictable. In a patient who is cold, vasoconstricted, edematous, or in shock, the same route may become slow and erratic. Topical medications are usually intended for local effect, whereas transdermal systems are designed for systemic delivery over time. Patients often treat those routes as interchangeable when they are not.

Heat is a particularly important transdermal teaching point. Fever, heating pads, warming blankets, or hot tubs can increase skin blood flow and increase drug absorption. A patient using a transdermal opioid patch who also applies direct heat may become oversedated even though the order itself was correct.

2.2.11 Nursing assessment questions that uncover absorption problems

When a medication seems ineffective or unpredictable, nurses can often clarify the problem with focused questions. Can the patient swallow safely? Has the patient been vomiting or having diarrhea? Was the dose taken with food, dairy, antacids, supplements, or tube feeds? Was a sublingual medication swallowed? Was a modified-release tablet crushed? Is the patient in shock or on vasopressors? These questions are often more useful than immediately assuming “the medication does not work.”

2.2.12 Bedside application

Absorption problems commonly explain three high-risk clinical patterns: delayed effect, absent effect, and unexpectedly rapid or intense effect. A post-op patient with nausea may not absorb an oral analgesic. A patient with dysphagia may receive a crushed ER product and experience toxicity. A patient using an inhaler incorrectly may look as though the bronchodilator failed when the real problem was delivery technique.

The core nursing takeaway is that absorption should always be considered before concluding that a dose was ineffective or that the drug itself is wrong. If the route, timing, formulation, GI condition, or technique changed, exposure may have changed too.

contentBlocks.title: High-Risk Distribution Checklist (Who Needs Extra Caution?)
contentBlocks.htmlReady:
Consider a distribution-related risk when a patient has:

  • Low albumin or obvious malnutrition
  • Advanced liver disease
  • Severe renal disease with uremia (binding competition)
  • Major edema, ascites, or large fluid shifts (aggressive IV fluids/diuresis)
  • Extremes of body composition (frail/cachectic, severe obesity)
  • Pregnancy or lactation considerations
  • Need for CNS penetration (CNS infection, seizure management, certain psychiatric goals)

If present: expect that standard doses may produce unexpected effects. Increase monitoring and communicate early when response is outside expectations.

sections.content:
2.3 Distribution — Where Drugs Go

After a drug reaches the bloodstream, it does not stay there evenly or indefinitely. Distribution is the movement of drug from the bloodstream into tissues, organs, and body compartments. This step helps explain why two patients can receive the same dose yet experience different effects. One patient may have a brisk clinical response; another may have little effect at first; a third may develop toxicity even though the ordered dose looks ordinary.

For nursing students, distribution is where pharmacokinetics starts to feel especially clinical. It connects medication response to perfusion, protein binding, body composition, fluid shifts, and physiologic barriers. In bedside language, distribution asks: Where is the drug actually going, how quickly is it getting there, and how much remains free to act?

2.3.1 Perfusion determines where drugs go first

Distribution begins with blood flow. Organs that receive high perfusion—such as the brain, heart, liver, and kidneys—are exposed to circulating drug earlier than tissues with lower perfusion, such as adipose tissue, skin, and resting muscle. This is why some medications produce rapid CNS or cardiovascular effects even though broader tissue distribution takes longer.

Perfusion also changes in illness. In shock, severe heart failure, or major blood loss, the body prioritizes blood flow to core organs. Peripheral tissues and the GI tract may receive much less. As a result, drug delivery to some tissues may be delayed, while clearance may also begin to fall. Later, when perfusion improves, the same patient may show delayed redistribution or accumulation effects that were not obvious at first.

2.3.2 Free drug versus protein-bound drug

Many drugs travel in the blood partly bound to plasma proteins and partly unbound. The most clinically important concept is that free (unbound) drug is generally the fraction that can leave the bloodstream, reach receptors, produce effect, and undergo filtration or metabolism. Protein-bound drug acts more like a temporary reservoir.

Albumin is the major binding protein for many acidic and neutral drugs, while alpha-1 acid glycoprotein is more important for some basic drugs. When protein binding falls, the free fraction may rise. That can increase effect and toxicity even if the total measured drug level looks unchanged.

2.3.3 Why low albumin matters clinically

Low albumin is common in malnutrition, liver disease, nephrotic states, critical illness, and some chronic inflammatory conditions. In patients receiving highly protein-bound drugs, reduced albumin can raise the free drug fraction and increase risk for exaggerated response or toxicity. The danger is not always obvious if clinicians focus only on the dose rather than the patient context.

For nurses, low albumin should trigger a more cautious mental model. A patient with advanced liver disease, poor nutrition, or severe illness may respond more strongly to a dose that previously seemed routine. If the medication is highly protein-bound or has a narrow therapeutic window, monitoring should be even more deliberate.

2.3.4 Body composition and fluid status change distribution

Distribution also depends on where the drug prefers to go. Lipophilic drugs tend to distribute more widely into fatty tissues, while hydrophilic drugs remain more confined to plasma and extracellular water. That means body composition matters. A frail patient with little muscle and limited protein reserve may not handle drugs the same way as a patient with obesity or substantial edema.

Large fluid shifts can be just as important. Edema, ascites, aggressive IV fluid resuscitation, burns, pregnancy, and third spacing can expand the apparent distribution space for hydrophilic medications. In practice, that may contribute to delayed achievement of effective concentrations or make dose interpretation less straightforward.

2.3.5 Volume of distribution: a useful concept, not a literal body space

Volume of distribution (Vd) is a conceptual measure that helps describe how extensively a drug leaves the bloodstream and enters tissues. A drug with a small Vd stays relatively concentrated in the plasma. A drug with a large Vd distributes widely into tissues. Nurses do not need to calculate Vd at the bedside for most medications, but they should understand its practical meaning.

A drug with a large Vd may require a loading dose in some situations to achieve a therapeutic concentration more quickly. It may also be less efficiently removed by dialysis, because much of the drug is not sitting in the bloodstream. A drug with a small Vd may produce more immediate vascular or plasma-related effects and may be more accessible to extracorporeal removal.

2.3.6 Physiologic barriers limit where drugs can go

Not every tissue is equally accessible. The blood-brain barrier (BBB) restricts entry of many substances into the central nervous system. The placenta allows some drugs to cross more easily than others. Local inflammation, meningitis, and other disease states can alter barrier permeability and change which drugs can reach a target site effectively.

This matters because drug choice is not based only on whether a medication is active against the disease process in theory. It must also reach the relevant site in practice. A medication may be highly effective in vitro yet still fail clinically if it does not penetrate the tissue where the problem exists.

2.3.7 Special populations and altered distribution

Several patient groups require extra attention. Older adults may have less total body water, more body fat, and lower albumin, which can alter both peak effect and duration. Patients with obesity may store lipophilic drugs differently and experience prolonged sedation or delayed release from tissue stores. Pregnant patients have increased plasma volume and altered body water distribution, while critically ill patients may have capillary leak, edema, hypoalbuminemia, and rapidly shifting perfusion.

These changes do not mean every dose must be reinvented from scratch, but they do mean nurses should be slower to assume that a standard dose will produce a standard response.

2.3.8 Clinical warning signs of a distribution problem

Distribution problems often present as a mismatch between the dose and the response. A patient with low albumin becomes more sedated than expected. A drug that should act centrally appears ineffective because it does not cross the BBB well enough. A patient with severe edema shows delayed effect with a hydrophilic medication. A patient who seemed undertreated during shock later develops signs of accumulation as organ perfusion improves and dosing continues.

When that mismatch appears, the nursing question is not only “Was the medication given?” It is also “Where did the medication likely go, and how much of it was actually free to act?”

2.3.9 Bedside nursing implications

Nurses rarely change distribution directly, but they constantly identify conditions that change it. Low albumin, malnutrition, obesity, ascites, large-volume fluid shifts, pregnancy, critical illness, and the need for CNS penetration should all sharpen monitoring. Documentation should include the pattern of response, not just the fact that the dose was administered.

Distribution is therefore one of the best examples of why pharmacokinetics matters in real care. The same order can produce a different exposure when the patient’s proteins, perfusion, fluid balance, or tissue barriers change. Seeing that early helps prevent both under-treatment and toxicity.

learningObjectives: Define metabolism and differentiate Phase I and Phase II processes at a high level.
Explain how CYP450 induction and inhibition produce clinically significant drug interactions.
Recognize patient and medication factors that increase metabolism-related adverse effects.
Apply interaction thinking to polypharmacy and new-start/stop medication changes.
keyTakeaways: Metabolism (often hepatic) makes drugs easier to eliminate but creates interaction risk.
CYP450 inhibitors raise drug levels; inducers lower drug levels.
Always reassess when medications are started, stopped, or doses are changed.

contentBlocks.title: Metabolism Safety Checklist (Before You Give the Next Dose)
contentBlocks.htmlReady:
• New med, stopped med, or dose change? Re-scan for CYP/P-gp interactions and update the timeline (hours vs. weeks).
  • Food/supplement check: grapefruit products, St. John’s wort, “detox” blends, energy drinks/herbals.
  • Liver vulnerability: known cirrhosis/hepatitis, jaundice, ascites, encephalopathy, low albumin, elevated INR.
  • High-risk substrates present? warfarin, statins, anticonvulsants, opioids/sedatives, immunosuppressants.
  • Look for early toxicity patterns: increasing sedation, bleeding/bruising, hypotension/bradycardia, new muscle pain/weakness.
  • Escalate with specifics: what changed, when, current symptoms, and what monitoring you recommend (INR, drug level, vitals).

sections.content:
2.4 Metabolism & CYP450

Metabolism, also called biotransformation, is the chemical alteration of a drug into one or more new forms. In most cases, metabolism makes a drug easier to eliminate by converting a relatively lipid-soluble molecule into a more water-soluble form. The liver is the major metabolic organ, but metabolism can also occur in the intestine, kidneys, lungs, and other tissues.

For nurses, metabolism becomes clinically important whenever the patient’s drug effect changes even though the dose did not. A new inhibitor may make the same dose act “too strong.” A new inducer may make the same dose appear ineffective. Liver disease, malnutrition, alcohol use, supplements, genetics, and drug-drug interactions can all shift medication exposure by altering metabolism.

2.4.1 Why metabolism matters to drug response

Many drugs are lipophilic enough to cross membranes easily, but that same property makes them harder to excrete in water-based urine. Metabolism helps solve that problem by preparing the drug for elimination. Sometimes metabolism inactivates the drug. Sometimes it activates a prodrug. Sometimes it creates active metabolites that continue to produce therapeutic or toxic effects long after the parent drug is given.

This is why nurses should think of metabolism as more than a chemistry step. It directly shapes onset, intensity, duration, interaction risk, and delayed toxicity.

2.4.2 Phase I and Phase II metabolism

Drug metabolism is often described in two broad stages. Phase I reactions usually involve oxidation, reduction, or hydrolysis. These reactions may inactivate a drug, activate a prodrug, or create a metabolite that still has pharmacologic activity. Phase II reactions usually involve conjugation, in which the drug or Phase I metabolite is linked to another chemical group to make it more water-soluble and easier to excrete.

Students do not need to memorize every pathway, but they should understand the logic: Phase I often modifies the molecule; Phase II often packages it for removal. Either phase can become clinically important if organ function changes or if interacting medications alter enzyme activity.

2.4.3 The cytochrome P450 system

The most famous Phase I pathway is the cytochrome P450 (CYP450) enzyme system, located primarily in the liver and intestinal wall. Several CYP enzymes are especially important in nursing pharmacology, including CYP3A4, CYP2D6, CYP2C9, CYP2C19, and CYP1A2. Different medications rely on different isoenzymes, which is why one interaction pattern does not apply equally to every drug.

The practical bedside point is simple: if a patient starts or stops a medication, supplement, smoking habit, or alcohol pattern that affects a CYP pathway, drug exposure may change even though the prescription itself did not change.

2.4.4 Enzyme inhibition: when drug levels rise

An enzyme inhibitor slows the metabolism of drugs that depend on that pathway. If the same dose continues while metabolism slows, drug concentrations may rise and adverse effects may emerge. The change can happen quickly, especially when the inhibitor is started abruptly.

In real practice, this means a patient who was stable on one medication may suddenly become oversedated, hypotensive, bradycardic, over-anticoagulated, or otherwise toxic after another medication is added. Nurses are often the first to see that timeline because they notice that “nothing changed except the new drug.” That observation can be highly valuable.

2.4.5 Enzyme induction: when drug levels fall

An enzyme inducer increases the metabolic capacity of a pathway over time, usually causing faster clearance of susceptible drugs and lower drug exposure. Unlike inhibition, induction is often delayed because new enzyme production takes time. The effect may emerge gradually over several days or longer.

This delay matters clinically. A medication may seem adequate when the interacting drug is first started, then become less effective later. Nurses should therefore watch for a gradual loss of therapeutic effect after a known inducer is introduced or discontinued.

2.4.6 Prodrugs and active metabolites

Not all metabolism reduces effect. Some drugs are administered in relatively inactive form and require metabolic conversion to become fully active. These are called prodrugs. If the relevant metabolic pathway is impaired or genetically reduced, the medication may appear weaker than expected. The reverse can also occur: a patient with unusually efficient activation may experience stronger effects or higher toxicity risk.

Other drugs produce active metabolites that continue to contribute to the clinical effect. In those situations, a patient may keep showing therapeutic benefit—or harm—even after the parent drug level begins to fall. This is one reason some adverse effects seem delayed or prolonged.

2.4.7 First-pass metabolism revisited

Metabolism also affects oral drug exposure before the drug ever reaches full systemic circulation. As described in Section 2.2, swallowed medications may undergo metabolism in the intestinal wall and liver during first pass. That means oral dosing must often be higher than IV dosing to achieve comparable exposure. It also means that route changes can significantly alter effect.

When nurses teach patients why one form of the same medication acts differently from another, first-pass metabolism is often part of the explanation.

2.4.8 Genetics and variable metabolism

Patients do not all metabolize drugs at the same rate. Genetic variation can alter enzyme activity, especially for pathways such as CYP2D6 and CYP2C19. Some patients are poor metabolizers and accumulate certain drugs more easily. Others are ultrarapid metabolizers and may clear or activate a drug much faster than expected.

Nursing students do not need full pharmacogenomics expertise at this stage, but they should recognize the pattern: if the dose is reasonable, adherence is good, organ function looks stable, and the patient still has an unusually strong or weak response, genetic variability may be part of the story.

2.4.9 Liver disease, nutrition, and clinical instability

Metabolism may be impaired by cirrhosis, hepatitis, reduced hepatic blood flow, malnutrition, inflammation, severe illness, and some endocrine states. Alcohol can complicate the picture further, sometimes by acutely inhibiting metabolism and sometimes by chronically inducing certain pathways depending on the situation. Herbal products and OTC supplements can also meaningfully alter metabolism.

For nurses, medication reconciliation is therefore part of pharmacokinetic safety. The question is not just “What prescriptions is the patient taking?” It is also “What else enters this patient’s body that could change metabolism?”

2.4.10 Bedside clues that point toward a metabolism problem

Common clinical clues include sudden oversedation after a new medication is added, unexpected INR change after an interacting drug starts, seizure recurrence after a metabolic inducer lowers antiepileptic exposure, or prolonged sedation in a patient with severe liver disease. These are not random events. They often reflect altered metabolism.

A useful nursing habit is to pair the symptom with the timeline. What changed recently? Did the patient start an antibiotic, antifungal, anticonvulsant, smoking cessation plan, supplement, or alcohol withdrawal? Did liver function worsen? That timeline often tells the story faster than memorizing isolated interaction lists.

2.4.11 Bedside nursing implications

Metabolism problems are rarely fixed by nurses alone, but they are often recognized by nurses first. Safe practice includes careful medication reconciliation, asking about OTC products and supplements, noticing new-response patterns after drug changes, monitoring liver-related risk, and escalating concerns using clinically meaningful language such as “possible accumulation,” “possible inhibitor effect,” or “possible loss of effect after inducer started.”

The broader lesson is that drug metabolism is dynamic. A dose that worked last month may become unsafe or ineffective when the patient’s liver function, genetics, interacting medications, or clinical condition change. Modern pharmacology requires nurses to watch for those shifts in real time.

contentBlocks.title: Renal Elimination Monitoring Checklist (High-Yield)
contentBlocks.htmlReady:
1. Trend kidney function: CrCl/eGFR + serum creatinine (look at direction, not one number).
  2. Track urine output: note declines, oliguria, and changes with fluids/diuretics.
  3. Assess volume status: dehydration, hypotension, heart failure exacerbation, overdiuresis.
  4. Review nephrotoxins: NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, vancomycin, contrast, ACEi/ARB during dehydration.
  5. Watch for accumulation signals: increasing sedation/confusion, nausea, arrhythmias, rising troughs.
  6. Escalate early: request pharmacy dosing review and clarify timing around dialysis if applicable.

sections.content:
2.5 Excretion & Drug Elimination

Excretion is the process by which drugs and their metabolites leave the body. In bedside practice, excretion is one of the most important reasons a previously safe dose can become unsafe. If elimination slows while dosing continues, the medication may accumulate and eventually produce exaggerated effects, toxicity, or delayed adverse reactions.

The kidneys are the major route of drug elimination for many medications, but they are not the only route. Hepatobiliary excretion, pulmonary excretion, and smaller routes such as sweat, saliva, and breast milk can also matter in selected situations. For nursing students, the highest-yield concept is simple: when organ function changes, drug exit changes.

2.5.1 Renal excretion is the main pathway for many drugs

The kidney eliminates many medications through three major processes: glomerular filtration, tubular secretion, and tubular reabsorption. Understanding these processes conceptually helps explain why renal disease, dehydration, age, and interactions can change drug exposure so quickly.

During glomerular filtration, small unbound drug molecules pass from the blood into the filtrate. Protein-bound drug generally does not filter well, which is why the free fraction matters. Tubular secretion actively moves certain drugs from blood into the tubular fluid. Tubular reabsorption moves some drug back into the bloodstream, especially when the molecule is lipid-soluble or conditions favor reentry.

2.5.2 Why the free fraction matters again

Only the unbound portion of a drug is readily filtered at the glomerulus. This links excretion directly to distribution and protein binding. A highly protein-bound drug may remain in the body longer because less of it is immediately available for filtration. That does not automatically make it safer. If binding changes later, the free fraction may rise and effects may become more pronounced.

2.5.3 Renal function is not static

Students sometimes learn renal dosing as though kidney function is a fixed trait, but in real care it can change quickly. Acute kidney injury, dehydration, sepsis, heart failure, nephrotoxic medications, obstruction, and contrast exposure may all reduce elimination over a short time. Chronic kidney disease lowers baseline reserve, making patients more vulnerable to accumulation when new stressors occur.

This is why nurses should think in trends, not isolated values. A dose that was reasonable two days ago may deserve clarification today if urine output has fallen, creatinine is rising, blood pressure has dropped, or the patient has become newly volume depleted.

2.5.4 Creatinine, eGFR, and creatinine clearance

Because direct measurement of renal drug clearance is impractical in routine care, clinicians often estimate kidney function using serum creatinine, estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR), or creatinine clearance (CrCl). These numbers help guide dosing and interval adjustments for renally cleared drugs, but they must still be interpreted in context.

For example, serum creatinine may appear deceptively “normal” in a frail older adult with low muscle mass, while true renal reserve is limited. Rapidly changing kidney function may also make a single estimate less reliable. Nurses do not need to calculate every value independently, but they should recognize when the trend suggests that the ordered regimen may no longer fit the patient.

2.5.5 Tubular secretion, reabsorption, and interaction risk

Not all renal elimination depends only on filtration. Some drugs rely heavily on active tubular secretion, and some may be reabsorbed more or less depending on urine conditions and molecular properties. Competing medications can sometimes slow secretion and raise drug levels. In selected cases, urine pH can influence reabsorption and elimination.

At the student level, the main takeaway is that renal elimination is more complex than “the kidneys filter it out.” Interactions and physiologic conditions can change excretion even when serum creatinine looks relatively stable.

2.5.6 Hepatobiliary and other excretion routes

Some drugs or metabolites are excreted into bile and leave the body through the stool. Others are exhaled through the lungs, as with some inhaled anesthetic agents or alcohol. Lactation also matters, because some medications enter breast milk and may affect the infant. These routes are less common than renal elimination in basic pharmacology discussions, but they remind students that excretion depends on the specific drug, not on a one-size-fits-all rule.

2.5.7 Dialysis and extracorporeal removal

Dialysis can remove some drugs effectively, but not all. Whether a medication is dialyzable depends on factors such as molecular size, protein binding, water solubility, and volume of distribution. Drugs that stay mostly in the plasma and are not heavily protein-bound are generally easier to remove than drugs that distribute widely into tissues.

For nurses, dialysis raises practical questions about timing, redosing, and monitoring. A medication may need to be scheduled around dialysis or supplemented afterward. If dialysis status changes, medication planning may need to change too.

2.5.8 Patient groups at high risk for excretion-related toxicity

Several groups deserve extra caution: older adults, patients with CKD or AKI, volume-depleted patients, critically ill patients with rapidly shifting organ function, and patients receiving other nephrotoxic or renally cleared medications. In these patients, the safest approach is not to assume that yesterday’s dose remains appropriate simply because the order is still active.

2.5.9 Clinical clues that suggest accumulation from poor elimination

Excretion problems often appear as a pattern rather than a single dramatic event. The patient becomes more sedated over successive doses. Digoxin toxicity emerges after renal function declines. An antibiotic causes worsening adverse effects after urine output falls. A patient develops confusion, tremor, hypotension, or prolonged drug action even though no dosing error is obvious.

When nurses see a progressive pattern, it is worth asking whether the drug is staying in the body longer than intended. That question frequently leads back to excretion.

2.5.10 Bedside nursing implications

Safe nursing care includes trending urine output, daily weights when relevant, BUN/creatinine or eGFR patterns, dialysis status, and fluid balance. It also includes noticing when the medication effect no longer matches the dose history. A helpful escalation statement is often more specific than “the patient seems off.” Try: “The patient’s urine output has dropped and creatinine is rising; I’m concerned this renally cleared medication may be accumulating.”

That kind of pharmacokinetic reasoning improves communication and supports earlier prevention of toxicity. Excretion is therefore not the end of the drug story in a trivial sense. It is the mechanism that determines whether repeated dosing remains therapeutic or slowly becomes unsafe.

sections.title: Section 2.6: Half-Life, Clearance & Steady State
learningObjectives: Define half-life and clearance and describe their relationship to dosing intervals.
Explain steady state and estimate time-to-steady-state using the 4–5 half-life rule.
Recognize accumulation risk when dosing frequency exceeds elimination capacity.
Apply timing concepts to monitoring (onset, peak, duration, therapeutic drug levels).
keyTakeaways: Most drugs take ~4–5 half-lives to approach steady state.
Long half-life + frequent dosing increases accumulation risk.
Timing knowledge improves monitoring, education, and safe dose adjustments.

contentBlocks.title: Therapeutic Drug Monitoring (TDM): Timing and Documentation
contentBlocks.htmlReady:
• Document exact times: last dose start/stop time (or administration time) and lab draw time.
  • Follow the protocol: troughs are drawn just before the next scheduled dose (per facility window).
  • Don’t improvise timing: an “off-time” level may be uninterpretable and lead to unsafe dose changes.
  • Trend with patient status: interpret levels alongside renal/hepatic function and clinical response.
  • Escalate when: rising levels, new toxicity signs, or sudden clearance changes (AKI, shock, new inhibitor/inducer).

sections.content:
2.6 Half-Life, Clearance & Steady State

Three pharmacokinetic ideas connect almost every dosing decision in practice: half-life, clearance, and steady state. These concepts help explain why some medications need frequent dosing, why others accumulate slowly, why dose changes may not show their full effect right away, and why toxicity can appear after several apparently uneventful doses.

For nursing students, this section is less about memorizing equations and more about learning how time shapes drug exposure. The key bedside question is simple: How long will this drug stay around, and what happens if the next dose arrives before enough of the previous one is gone?

2.6.1 Half-life: the time signal behind dosing

Half-life (t½) is the time required for the plasma concentration of a drug to decrease by about 50%. It is one of the most useful concepts in pharmacokinetics because it helps predict duration of action, dosing interval, time to washout, and the pace of accumulation.

A short half-life usually means the drug leaves the body relatively quickly, so doses may need to be given more often to maintain effect. A long half-life means the drug remains in the body much longer, which may support less frequent dosing but also increases the risk that effects or adverse effects will linger after a dose change.

2.6.2 Half-life depends on volume of distribution and clearance

Conceptually, half-life reflects the relationship between how widely the drug distributes and how efficiently the body removes it. If a drug distributes widely into tissues or if clearance falls, half-life often becomes longer. If clearance rises, half-life may shorten.

That is why half-life is not just a property printed in a drug guide. In real patients, half-life can shift when renal function declines, liver disease worsens, perfusion changes, or interactions alter metabolism.

2.6.3 Clearance: the body’s drug-removal capacity

Clearance (CL) describes the volume of plasma from which drug is effectively removed per unit time. In practical nursing language, clearance reflects how well the body can get rid of the drug. It is influenced mainly by renal excretion and hepatic metabolism, though other routes may matter for certain medications.

Reduced clearance does not always cause an immediate problem after one dose. The more common pattern is quieter and more dangerous: the same maintenance dose continues, but the drug now leaves more slowly, so concentration rises over time. That is the classic setup for accumulation.

2.6.4 Accumulation happens when dosing outpaces elimination

When repeated doses are given before the body has eliminated enough of the previous doses, drug concentration builds. This is normal to some extent during maintenance therapy and is often necessary to achieve a therapeutic level. The problem begins when accumulation overshoots the intended therapeutic window.

At the bedside, accumulation often explains delayed toxicity. A patient may tolerate the first dose well, seem acceptable after the second, and then become obviously overexposed after the third or fourth dose because the body never fully cleared the earlier doses. Renal decline, hepatic impairment, long half-life, and interacting medications all make this more likely.

2.6.5 Steady state: when drug in equals drug out

Steady state is the point at which the average amount of drug entering the body over time equals the average amount being eliminated. At steady state, the concentration still rises and falls within each dosing interval, but the overall pattern becomes relatively stable from one interval to the next.

A high-yield rule is that many drugs reach steady state after about four to five half-lives if dosing remains consistent. This rule explains why some medications do not show their full maintenance effect immediately and why early dose adjustments can be misleading.

2.6.6 Why “wait and reassess” is sometimes correct

Students are often tempted to interpret a partial response after one or two doses as proof that the regimen has failed. Sometimes that is true. But for medications that require several half-lives to approach steady state, the response may still be evolving appropriately. Premature upward titration can overshoot the target and create toxicity once the accumulated level catches up.

This is especially important for long-half-life medications and for patients with impaired clearance. Nurses help prevent harm by recognizing that pharmacokinetics may require patience, not immediate escalation.

2.6.7 Loading dose versus maintenance dose

A loading dose is a larger initial dose used to reach a therapeutic concentration more quickly when waiting multiple half-lives would be unsafe or impractical. A maintenance dose is the ongoing dose designed to replace what the body eliminates and keep the patient near the target exposure over time.

From a nursing perspective, the logic matters more than the formula. If a drug has a large volume of distribution or a long half-life, a loading dose may be used to “fill the space” more quickly. That does not mean the patient should continue receiving large doses indefinitely. Once the target is reached, maintenance dosing takes over.

2.6.8 Peak, trough, and timing of assessment

Half-life and steady state also help explain why timing matters for drug levels and clinical assessment. A peak level reflects the highest concentration in a dosing cycle, while a trough reflects the lowest concentration before the next dose. If levels are drawn at the wrong time, the result may be difficult or impossible to interpret correctly.

Nurses therefore play a crucial role in accurate monitoring. Correct timing of lab draws, clear documentation of last-dose time, and awareness of changing kidney or liver function all make therapeutic drug monitoring more meaningful.

2.6.9 First-order versus zero-order thinking

Most drugs follow first-order kinetics, meaning a constant fraction of drug is eliminated per unit time. In that situation, half-life is a practical and reasonably stable concept. A smaller number of medications can approach zero-order behavior at certain concentrations, meaning a constant amount rather than a constant fraction is eliminated over time. When that happens, small dose increases may produce disproportionately large rises in concentration.

Students do not need advanced kinetics for most bedside work, but they should remember that not every drug behaves perfectly predictably at higher concentrations. If the medication is known to have a narrow therapeutic window, monitoring becomes even more important.

2.6.10 Clinical situations where these concepts matter most

Half-life, clearance, and steady state are especially important with narrow therapeutic index drugs, sedatives, anticonvulsants, antiarrhythmics, anticoagulants, renally cleared antibiotics, and medications used in older adults or critically ill patients. These are the situations where timing errors, delayed accumulation, or premature dose adjustments are most likely to cause harm.

2.6.11 Bedside nursing implications

When a medication response changes, nurses should ask several timing-based questions. When was the last dose given? Has the drug had enough time to reach effect? Could repeated dosing have caused accumulation? Has clearance changed because of AKI, liver dysfunction, dehydration, or interactions? Is the current level or symptom pattern consistent with peak effect, trough effect, or steady-state accumulation?

Those questions often turn vague concern into a useful clinical hypothesis. Half-life, clearance, and steady state are therefore not abstract equations from a pharmacology lecture. They are practical tools for deciding when to wait, when to monitor more closely, and when to escalate concern that the patient is drifting outside the therapeutic window.

contentBlocks.title: Nursing Monitoring Checklist for PK Risk (Special Populations)
contentBlocks.htmlReady:
Before giving the dose

  • Confirm the scalar: correct patient weight (kg), correct dosing weight (TBW/IBW/AdjBW if specified), and correct units.
  • Confirm the context: pregnancy trimester/gestational age, lactation, age/frailty cues, renal/hepatic trends, dialysis/CRRT modality and timing.
  • Check route reliability: vomiting, ileus, shock, edema (SubQ), poor perfusion (IM) — consider whether absorption is trustworthy.
  • Scan for new interactions: new antibiotics/antifungals/macrolides, anticonvulsants, supplements/herbals, tube feeds, antacids/iron/calcium.

After giving the dose

  • Trend the right endpoints: vitals, sedation/respiratory status, pain score/function, mental status, bleeding, QTc (if relevant), urine output.
  • Time-stamp precisely: record exact administration time; therapeutic drug levels are only interpretable with accurate timing.
  • Watch for delayed patterns: toxicity after day 2–5 suggests accumulation; loss of effect after several days suggests induction or increased clearance.

When to escalate immediately

  • New/worsening respiratory depression, severe sedation, hypotension, active bleeding, seizures, or significant arrhythmia/QTc change.
  • Rapid renal/hepatic deterioration or initiation/change in dialysis/CRRT settings.
  • Unexpected nonresponse in a time-sensitive condition (sepsis, status epilepticus, severe pain crisis) despite adherence.

SBAR tip: pair the symptom with the timing (“after dose #6”) and the special-population factor (“CRRT started today”).

sections.content:
2.7 Special Populations & PK Adjustments

A medication order includes a dose, route, and schedule, but pharmacokinetics is the reason those details are rarely “one size fits all.” Special populations are patient groups in which one or more parts of ADME are predictably different. The nurse’s job is not to memorize every adjustment for every drug. The nurse’s job is to recognize when a standard order may expose a patient to subtherapeutic treatment, delayed toxicity, or a preventable adverse drug event.

A useful starting question is: Which part of ADME is most likely altered in this patient, and what data do I need before I give the next dose?

2.7.1 A bedside framework: ADME + patient story

Pharmacokinetics becomes easier to apply when it is attached to the patient’s story rather than treated as a separate science topic. The same medication can behave differently because of:

  • Absorption changes such as vomiting, diarrhea, tube feeds, altered gastric pH, or unreliable GI function
  • Distribution changes such as edema, ascites, obesity, cachexia, low albumin, or reduced perfusion
  • Metabolism changes such as liver disease, genetic variation, alcohol use, or a new inhibitor/inducer
  • Excretion changes such as acute kidney injury, chronic kidney disease, dehydration, or dialysis

When you see words like neonate, pregnant, older adult, frail, cirrhosis, obesity, malnutrition, or critical illness, that should trigger immediate PK thinking.

2.7.2 Pediatrics: development changes every part of PK

Pediatric patients are not simply smaller adults. Development affects absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion, and the safety margin is narrower because many pediatric doses are weight based.

Absorption may be less predictable because gastric pH, gastric emptying, GI motility, skin characteristics, and perfusion differ by age. IM and subcutaneous absorption can be unreliable in a sick or poorly perfused child.

Distribution differs because infants have higher total body water, lower body fat early in life, and less mature protein binding. Water-soluble drugs may distribute more widely, while highly protein-bound drugs may have a higher free fraction.

Metabolism also changes across development. Some hepatic pathways are immature in neonates, while some children later metabolize certain drugs more rapidly than adults.

Excretion is especially important in neonates and young infants because renal function is immature and glomerular filtration rises over time. A renally cleared drug can accumulate quickly if the interval is not adjusted appropriately.

Nursing implications:

  • Verify the most current weight in kilograms, not pounds.
  • Check the full dosing chain: mg/kg → total dose → concentration → volume to administer.
  • Remember that small volume errors can become major overdoses in infants.
  • Monitor using developmentally appropriate cues, especially when a child cannot describe dizziness, sedation, tinnitus, or nausea clearly.

Mini-case: A neonate receiving gentamicin may require special attention to interval timing, drug levels, urine output, and creatinine trends because immature renal clearance can turn a routine order into an accumulation risk.

2.7.3 Pregnancy and lactation: PK changes plus fetal/infant safety

Pregnancy affects pharmacokinetics in several ways. Plasma volume expands, albumin concentration may fall, body fat changes, GI symptoms can alter oral intake, and glomerular filtration often increases. The result is that the same pre-pregnancy dose may lead to a different drug exposure during pregnancy.

Absorption may be altered by nausea, vomiting, delayed gastric emptying, or reduced medication tolerance.

Distribution changes with increased plasma volume, altered body composition, and lower albumin, which can affect free-drug fraction.

Metabolism changes are drug specific because some CYP pathways are induced while others are inhibited.

Excretion may increase for renally cleared medications because GFR often rises during pregnancy.

Pregnancy adds a second layer of decision-making: fetal safety. A medication may be pharmacokinetically reasonable for the mother yet still inappropriate because of teratogenicity or adverse fetal effects. Trimester matters, indication matters, and “leftover medication from last time” is never a safe shortcut.

Lactation requires a similar practical approach. Nurses should consider whether the drug enters breast milk, how much infant exposure is likely, the medication’s half-life, and whether the infant is full term or premature. Premature infants and very young infants have less mature metabolism and excretion, so even medications considered compatible with breastfeeding may require closer review.

Nursing implications:

  • Screen pregnancy status when clinically relevant before giving potentially harmful medications.
  • Use trusted pregnancy/lactation references rather than assumptions or memory alone.
  • Teach patients not to self-select old prescriptions or OTC products without guidance.
  • Ask breastfeeding patients about infant sedation, feeding difficulty, rash, diarrhea, or unusual sleepiness.
2.7.4 Older adults: accumulation can hide behind “normal” numbers

Older adults often experience multiple PK changes at once, along with increased pharmacodynamic sensitivity to many CNS-active and cardiovascular medications. The combination raises the risk of falls, delirium, bleeding, hypotension, and prolonged sedation.

Distribution changes with lower total body water, higher body fat, and sometimes lower albumin. Lipid-soluble drugs may linger longer, and highly protein-bound drugs may have a higher active free fraction when albumin is reduced.

Metabolism may be slower because hepatic blood flow and liver function reserve can decline with age and comorbid disease.

Excretion is one of the highest-yield concerns. Renal clearance often decreases with age, but serum creatinine can still look deceptively normal when muscle mass is low.

Nursing implications:

  • Look at trends in eGFR, creatinine clearance, urine output, and hydration status rather than a single creatinine value.
  • Watch for accumulation patterns: fine after early doses, then progressively sleepy, confused, hypotensive, or unstable.
  • Treat polypharmacy as a real clinical risk, especially when multiple sedating, anticholinergic, or bleeding-risk medications overlap.

Mini-case: An 82-year-old with frailty and “normal” creatinine who becomes increasingly sedated after repeated doses of a renally cleared medication should be assessed for hidden renal decline and accumulation, not simply labeled “sensitive.”

2.7.5 Renal and hepatic impairment: when maintenance doses become unsafe

Renal impairment often alters pharmacokinetics first because excretion is the final step for many drugs and metabolites. Chronic kidney disease, dehydration, sepsis, heart failure, obstruction, or acute kidney injury can all reduce clearance. When clearance falls, half-life lengthens and the same maintenance regimen can produce higher steady-state concentrations.

What to assess in renal impairment:

  • Serum creatinine trends, eGFR or creatinine clearance, and urine output
  • Recent dehydration, diuresis, nephrotoxins, contrast exposure, or dialysis schedule
  • Whether the ordered medication or its metabolites are primarily renally cleared
  • Whether the patient is showing early toxicity such as confusion, oversedation, hypotension, arrhythmias, or worsening GI effects

Hepatic impairment creates a different pattern. Some drugs are poorly metabolized when hepatic blood flow, hepatocyte function, or enzyme activity declines. Low albumin may also increase the free fraction of highly protein-bound drugs, making the patient appear unusually sensitive even when the total dose seems ordinary.

Nursing implications:

  • Do not assume “same dose as yesterday” is still appropriate after major lab or clinical change.
  • Communicate new oliguria, rising creatinine, new jaundice, worsening encephalopathy, or unexpected bleeding/sedation promptly.
  • Use SBAR language tied to PK: “I’m concerned this patient is accumulating the medication because renal function declined and sedation is increasing.”
2.7.6 Obesity, malnutrition, and critical illness: distribution becomes less predictable

Body composition and severity of illness can also shift pharmacokinetics significantly. In obesity, lipophilic drugs may distribute differently, and dosing may require clarification about actual, ideal, or adjusted body weight. In malnutrition or severe chronic illness, low albumin can increase free-drug exposure for highly protein-bound medications.

Critical illness adds even more variability. Shock, vasopressor use, capillary leak, edema, ascites, aggressive fluid resuscitation, and organ dysfunction can alter every step of ADME at once. In these patients, PK is dynamic rather than fixed.

Nursing takeaway: special populations are not a side topic in pharmacology. They are where PK becomes clinically visible. When the patient story changes, assume drug handling may have changed too, and reassess before harm occurs.

Timing and trend documentation make medication safety decisions safer.
